summary: Gruber examines Mark Gurman's Bloomberg rundown of Apple's executive leadership, noting it works better as a directory than a narrative story. He pushes back on the idea that Jeff Williams is Tim Cook's heir apparent, since the two are effectively the same age. The real substance of the piece is Gruber's deeper concern: with Jony Ive gone and Phil Schiller moved to a fellowship role, it's unclear who at Apple is the singular taste-driven voice directing product decisions. Gruber argues that great products, like great movies, cannot be directed by committee.
key_points: Jeff Williams is clearly second-in-command but shouldn't be seen as Cook's heir apparent since they're nearly the same age
Most of Apple's rising leaders can be identified by their keynote stage time, but some remain under the radar
With Ive departed and Schiller in a reduced role, there's a vacuum in singular product taste and direction at Apple
Apple still hasn't named a replacement for PR chief Steve Dowling after a full year, suggesting the role may fold into Greg Joswiak's marketing org
money_quotes: My biggest question and deepest concern regarding Apple's leadership, especially now that Ive is gone and Phil Schiller has moved on to a fellowship, is whose taste is driving product development?
We know the actors, we know the writers, we know the cinematographers, but who is directing?
Who is saying 'This isn't good enough' — or in the words of Apple's former director, 'This is shit'?
You can't direct good movies by committee. You can't direct good products by committee, either.
When a product decision comes down to this or that, who is making that call?
I feel like it's better thought of (and would have been better presented) as a directory, not as a story.
key_insight: The most important unanswered question about Apple's leadership isn't succession planning — it's who serves as the singular arbiter of taste and product direction now that Ive and Schiller are gone.
